data.title: A $2 billion reassurance: While embracing the AI ​​wave, a16z hasn't forgotten Crypto.
data.description:
Author: Yangz, Techub News

Amidst the fluctuating crypto market and a flood of capital rushing into the AI ​​sector, a major piece of news has emerged in the crypto venture capital field. According to Fortune magazine, citing multiple sources familiar with the matter, a16z crypto, the crypto division of a16z, is raising its fifth fund, targeting approximately $2 billion, and plans to complete fundraising by the end of the first half of 2026.

As a leading player in the crypto VC field, a16z's counter-cyclical fundraising has quickly attracted widespread market attention.

While the $2 billion figure is less than half of the $4.5 billion peak reached in 2022, at a crucial juncture of industry cycle rotation and a gradually clarifying regulatory environment, this move undoubtedly injects a strong boost into the crypto market during a downturn, and reaffirms its firm bet on the long-term prospects of blockchain technology. From $300 Million to $4.5 Billion: a16z's First Four Cycles of Bets To understand a16z Crypto's fifth fund's contrarian move, it's helpful to first look back at the evolution of its previous four funds. This is not only a history of capital expansion, but also an investment chronicle deeply intertwined with the crypto market cycle. **First Fund (June 2018): Sowing Seeds in a Bear Market.** At that time, Bitcoin had just fallen from its $20,000 high, the market was in a deep freeze, and mainstream capital was withdrawing. a16z, however, went against the trend and officially established its first dedicated crypto fund with a scale of $300 million. In its early stages, the fund heavily invested in MakerDAO (now renamed Sky), a veteran DeFi protocol, demonstrating its profound commitment to decentralized governance. This precise "counter-cyclical" move not only brought it substantial paper returns but also solidified a16z's unshakeable position in the Web3 field.

Second Fund (April 2020): On the Eve of Recovery。 On the eve of global financial turmoil and the outbreak of the "DeFi Summer", a16z launched its second fund of $515 million. Giants that would later dominate the DeFi sector, such as Uniswap, entered its portfolio during this period. In this phase, a16z adhered to its "DeFi Lego" strategy, betting on foundational protocols that could be combined to build a decentralized financial system: trading (Uniswap), lending (Compound), derivatives (Synthetix), and stablecoins (Celo), essentially covering the entire business line of decentralized banking.

Third Fund (June 2021): Bull Market Frenzy。 As the 2021 crypto bull market reached its climax, a16z raised a massive $2.2 billion, breaking the industry's fundraising record at the time. During this period, a16z's investment logic entered a "full-track expansion" mode, focusing on high-performance public chains such as Solana and Avalanche, leading NFT projects such as Yuga Labs and OpenSea, and DeFi protocols such as dYdX and Lido. At this point, a16z had undoubtedly evolved from a "track bettor" into a "super incubator" of the Web3 ecosystem. The fourth fund (May 2022): Peak Watch. Just as Terra collapsed and the market was in turmoil, a16z announced the completion of a massive $4.5 billion fund, shocking the industry. Of this, $1.5 billion was allocated to seed funding and $3 billion to venture capital. With this ample "winter provisions," a16z strategically built core infrastructure such as LayerZero and Optimism during the downturn, and also invested in Farcaster and Lens Protocol, two then highly anticipated decentralized social protocols. Chris Dixon's assertion at the time that "we are now entering the golden age of Web3" is still frequently cited today. From 300 million to 4.5 billion, from sowing seeds in a bear market to guarding the peak, a16z crypto's every fundraising round has coincided with different cyclical milestones, yet it has consistently adhered to the same logic: scale expansion stems from industry growth, investment themes deepen with technological iterations, and its resolve to weather cycles has never wavered. Now, its fifth fund may return to the market with a size of $2 billion. Unlike the previous "gradual leaps," this time the scale contraction and increased focus may indicate a16z's new assessment of the next cycle.

AI Siphon: Holding the fort in the home turf

Looking at Silicon Valley in early 2026, capital's focus has shifted dramatically towards AI. Just as Paradigm expanded the investment scope of its new $1.5 billion fund from pure crypto to artificial intelligence, robotics, and other fields, funds that once belonged to the Web3 world are being taken away by computing power and large-scale models.

Even more poignant is the departure of Kyle Samani, co-founder of Multicoin Capital. This standard-bearer, once hailed as one of the "greatest investors in crypto history," has shifted his focus to fields such as artificial intelligence and robotics.

data.title: Trump’s Ex-Advisor Calls Out Bitcoin Hypocrisy: “Loving BTC Isn’t Enough”
data.description:
From Bitcoin Promises to Political Inaction

Former crypto advisor David Bailey is publicly calling out the gap between Washington’s pro-Bitcoin rhetoric and real policy action, warning that political promises alone will not drive adoption.

Talks on landmark crypto legislation have hit a new impasse after banks said they could not back a compromise pushed by the White House, a development that cast doubt on whether the bill will pass this year and sparked criticism from President Trump who accused lenders of trying…

— Reuters Business (@ReutersBiz) March 5, 2026

Bailey, who previously advised Donald Trump on digital assets, said the United States risks stalling Bitcoin’s growth if leaders continue praising the asset without implementing concrete strategies.

In March 2025, Trump signed an executive order aimed at creating a strategic Bitcoin reserve, a move widely seen as a symbolic endorsement of crypto at the federal level. But one year later, Bailey says the initiative has produced little meaningful progress.

The United States already holds roughly 328,372 BTC, seized through law-enforcement operations and now worth more than $23 billion. Yet those holdings remain largely idle due to the absence of a clear policy framework or coordinated political effort to deploy them.

Bailey summed up the frustration bluntly: “Loving Bitcoin is not enough.” Without serious political commitment and the resources needed to turn policy into action, he argues, government enthusiasm for the asset risks becoming little more than empty rhetoric.

Global Competition Raises the Stakes

The warning comes as other countries move faster to integrate Bitcoin into their financial systems. El Salvador, for example, has already embedded Bitcoin into its national economic strategy — a stark contrast to what Bailey describes as Washington’s slower and more cautious approach.

Kids paying for McDonalds with Bitcoin in El Salvador 🇸🇻

The future is bright! 🙌 pic.twitter.com/3Gb13WBP2i

— Bitcoin Magazine (@BitcoinMagazine) February 17, 2026

Bitcoin itself remains volatile, trading around $70,000 in early 2026, but many analysts remain optimistic about its long-term prospects. Bailey believes that meaningful regulatory progress — including legislation such as the CLARITY Act — could accelerate institutional adoption and provide the clarity the market has long demanded.

Kids paying for McDonalds with Bitcoin in El Salvador 🇸🇻

The future is bright! 🙌 pic.twitter.com/3Gb13WBP2i

— Bitcoin Magazine (@BitcoinMagazine) February 17, 2026

With Bitcoin increasingly becoming a political issue ahead of future elections, Bailey’s message is clear: the next phase of crypto adoption will depend less on campaign rhetoric and more on whether governments are willing to translate pro-Bitcoin messaging into real policy.

data.title: Grayscale: The crypto market is stabilizing; three reasons for optimism about the future.
data.description:
Source: Grayscale; Compiled by: Jinse Finance

Key Takeaways

  • In early February, the market capitalization of cryptocurrencies declined, but stabilized by the end of the month. Trading volume, implied volatility, and other indicators also showed that market sentiment was calming down.

  • Crypto assets related to AI agents led the performance; stablecoins, asset tokenization, and the "3P domain"—Privacy, Perpetual futures, and Prediction Markets—all saw positive fundamental news.

  • We believe that now may be a suitable time for long-term investors to increase their allocation to crypto assets.

  • Blockchain and AI are complementary, the industry fundamentals remain positive, and the macro environment also provides support. The progress of the US Senate's Clarity Act remains an uncertainty. In early February, the crypto market fell along with high-growth software stocks and other early-stage US tech stocks. Innovation in the field of artificial intelligence appears to be accelerating, putting pressure on some business-related assets facing potential disruption. For example, at the end of January, Anthropic launched a plugin for its Claude Cowork tool, applicable to professional business service scenarios such as human resources, compliance, and legal. Blockchain is not a competitor to AI—we believe the two technologies are complementary, as will be explained below—but the risk-averse behavior of growth portfolios is likely to spill over into crypto assets (see Figure 1). Figure 1: Bitcoin Price Stabilizes After Early February Decline. [Image: https://img.jinse.com.cn/7437451_watermarknone.png] In late February, the market stabilized in terms of price performance, trading volume, and other indicators. For example, the market capitalization-weighted FTSE/Grayscale Crypto Sector Index fell 26% between January 30 and February 5, then rebounded by about 4% by the end of the month. Spot trading volume and implied volatility in options surged during the decline before falling back at the end of the month (see Figure 2), and open interest in perpetual contracts also stabilized. Figure 2: Bitcoin trading volume surges during market downturns. [Image of image: https://img.jinse.com.cn/7437452_watermarknone.png] Reasons for increasing holdings: Bitcoin and other crypto assets have experienced significant pullbacks, and short-term uncertainty remains high. However, we believe that for long-term investors, this is a good time to consider allocating capital. Our relatively optimistic core reasons are threefold: 1. Blockchain and AI are complementary. Large language models and public blockchain technology are not in competition: Blockchain aims to replace trusted financial intermediaries with cryptography and economic incentive systems. In fact, blockchain has specific advantages over the traditional banking system and is expected to become the underlying financial infrastructure for AI agents—Citrini Research's popular report has already discussed the potential disruption brought by AI. In this round of adjustments, crypto assets fell along with software stocks, but investors may more carefully distinguish between technologies that compete with and complement AI in the future. 2. The major trends of stablecoins and asset tokenization remain unchanged. Increased regulatory clarity (including the passage of the Genius Act last year) is driving institutional funds into stablecoins and tokenized assets—the two core application scenarios of public blockchains. In February, news emerged that Meta, after shelving its Libra/Diem project due to regulatory pressure, might be repositioning itself for stablecoins; Stripe stated in its annual open letter, "Stablecoin payments are quietly and irreversibly evolving as real-world adoption continues to accelerate." Furthermore, BlackRock announced the integration of its tokenized money market fund, BUIDL, with UniswapX.

    3. Macroeconomic Environment Favorable for Risk Assets

    The US economy has a solid fundamental foundation, with some high-frequency indicators showing accelerating growth. Overinvestment in AI poses a medium-term risk, but the pace of innovation remains rapid, and there is still a capacity gap in data centers. The market reacted negatively to Kevin Warsh's nomination to succeed Jerome Powell as Federal Reserve Chairman, but we believe his actual policy stance is unlikely to be as hawkish as some of his views during his tenure as a Federal Reserve Governor from 2006 to 2011.

    The fate of the US Senate's Clarity Act is a key variable for the short-term market outlook.

    In January, the Senate Banking Committee delayed the scheduled markup process for the bill, causing a major setback for the legislation. Subsequently, the White House held three meetings with the crypto and banking industries. According to media reports, the third meeting on February 19th had a smaller scope and more focused discussion, and produced a concrete draft text on the thorny issue of stablecoin yields. The Clarity Act provides a traditional financial regulatory framework for many areas of the digital asset industry, and we believe its passage is likely to drive institutional funds into this asset class. Meanwhile, regulatory clarity is progressing on multiple fronts, not just with a single bill. Even without the Clarity Act, the industry can continue to develop with more rules being established by regulators. However, in our view, the best-case scenario is still the passage of this bipartisan bill, formally enshrining digital asset regulation in written law. AI and 3P From the perspective of crypto industry sectors, the best-performing sub-sector in February was AI-related, with a smaller pullback than other sectors (see Figure 3). The excess returns primarily stemmed from a renewed market enthusiasm for AI agents (independent software capable of autonomously performing complex goals on behalf of users). Technological innovation in agent-based systems continued to accelerate, particularly with the locally deployed productivity assistant OpenClaw, which became one of the fastest-growing open-source projects in history. Leading projects within the AI ​​crypto sector included Kite AI, a blockchain built for native agent stablecoin payments, and Pippin AI, which provides infrastructure for on-chain AI agents. Figure 3: AI and Crypto Sector Leads Performance in February. [Image of image: https://img.jinse.com.cn/7437453_watermarknone.png] Within the entire digital asset ecosystem, there have been numerous advancements in the "3P areas" of privacy, perpetual futures, and prediction markets. For example, Near, also within the AI ​​and crypto sector, announced several initiatives combining AI and privacy, including introducing confidential transactions for its popular intents feature and providing support for AI agents with built-in privacy and encryption guarantees. Furthermore, the perpetual futures exchange Hyperliquid continues to expand beyond native crypto assets (HIP-3 perpetual futures). On January 30th, Hyperliquid's silver futures trading volume accounted for approximately 2% of CME COMEX silver futures, indicating that this market is large enough to warrant attention alongside traditional exchanges, especially during non-traditional trading hours. In addition to commodities, Hyperliquid has launched global stock futures. If liquidity continues to accumulate, its contracts may gradually become a venue for 24/7 trading and hedging of some global macro assets. During the Super Bowl on February 8th, prediction market platforms Polymarket and Kalshi saw another surge in trading volume (see Figure 4). More notably, trading volume remained high in the weeks that followed. These platforms have integrated into the news and information ecosystem, maintaining sustained activity beyond major events. Last month, Polymarket filed a trademark application for its POLY token, and its Chief Marketing Officer had previously hinted at an upcoming token launch. Meanwhile, the Federal Reserve released an analysis report affirming the value of prediction markets in predicting economic variables such as CPI inflation. Despite progress, these platforms still face regulatory questions: In February, the U.S. Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) reaffirmed its federal-only regulatory authority and intervened in litigation against states regulating them as unlicensed gambling. Figure 4: Prediction market trading volume remains high after the Super Bowl. After a significant correction since last October, the crypto market is showing signs of stabilization. Stablecoins and tokenized assets are expected to continue driving industry adoption, while innovations in areas such as AI, privacy, perpetual contracts, and prediction markets are progressing steadily. If the fundamental trend remains positive, crypto asset valuations will eventually recover.

data.title: AI Usage Guide for Humanities Workers
data.description:
Author: Han Yang MasterPa

Humanities workers don't create world change, but they are at the forefront of it.

Sometimes I feel that those accounts selling AI tutorials always treat AI as magic: giving you a magical prompt, and you can do anything. Reality, of course, isn't like that. For a period of time, because of founding FUNES, we had to produce a large amount of content daily using AI. In addition, there was the production of content such as *Ephemeral World* and my own writing, which meant that manpower alone was no longer enough. Therefore, we experimented extensively with how to use AI to assist our content marketing and humanities research.

Later, when new colleagues joined the company, I made a simple keynote. After hearing about this, Mr. Jia Xingjia from another platform invited me to give a presentation. My partner, Keda, and I named this presentation "A Guide to Using AI for Humanities Workers."

It was initially a purely private sharing session, mainly focusing on general principles. We did it several more times afterward, gradually expanding upon it. However, this sharing session had never been held publicly until this year when we launched the program "Poetry Combing the Wind" with Chongqing, so we had the first complete public discussion. The following text is compiled from the podcast "AI Usage Guide for Humanities Workers," with AI-assisted organization and some abbreviations. Over the past year, Keda and I have shared this experience on how to use AI with many friends who work in content creation, research, and knowledge product development. Its goal isn't to teach you to memorize a few magic prompts, nor is it to treat AI as a panacea; rather, it's more like a working method: allowing you to truly integrate large models into your writing, research, editing, topic selection, data organization, and production processes without writing code, and achieving **traceability, supervision, and verification**, so that you still want to be credited for your work. This approach stems from lessons learned in real-world projects: when content is mass-produced, relying solely on human labor will collapse; while having AI write an article directly can lead to illusions, laziness, and AI-like writing. Therefore, we had to transform creation into a production line, and the production line into an iterative system. Today, I don't want to give you various prompts directly; instead, I want to provide some key guiding principles and guidelines. Before the principles: Three bottom lines of this guide. Before discussing specific methods, let's clarify three bottom lines. They determine "how you use AI" and "why you use it this way." The process must be traceable, supervised, and verifiable. You cannot simply aim for a result and ignore the process. For humanities work, the black box is the most dangerous: illusions, misinterpretations, and misrepresentations can all quietly occur within it. It must be controllable. You need to be able to control how it's done, by what standards, where to slow down, and where to be strict. You're not "drawing cards," you're producing. Ultimately, you're still willing to sign your name. "Am I willing to put my name on it?" is the final quality check. If you're unwilling to sign your name, it's usually not a moral issue, but rather that your will wasn't implemented throughout the process—meaning the quality is uncontrollable.

Principle 0: Don't make wishes on AI, treat it as a workbench

Many people use AI in essence by making wishes: "Give me a good joke," "Help me write a good article," "Explain this paper."

The problem is—"Explanation" itself has countless interpretations: for laymen, for undergraduates, for graduate students, for colleagues, it's not a single task. AI cannot know your background, purpose, tastes, and standards by default. If you don't clarify, it can only give you the easiest answer using the default method of "average human."

Using a large model as a workbench means that you don't demand results from it, but rather use its tools to complete a process. Your task is to clearly define the task, the standards, and the steps. For example, let AI explain a paper. You can turn a wish-like request (explain this paper to me) into a workbench-style task like this: **Define your target audience:** Intelligent, curious graduate students who are not experts in the field. **Define your explanation style:** Heuristic, step-by-step, and academically rigorous. **Define your structure:** First, explain the significance, then provide background information, then reconstruct the research process, then explain the key technical points, and finally offer insights. **Define your tone:** Respect intelligence, avoid being condescending, and don't pretend the listener already has a deep understanding. You'll find that the more you present it like an "assignment requirement," the less AI it becomes, and the more it resembles a teaching assistant who can actually get things done. Principle 1: To make AI work well, first reflect on yourself—you are the person in charge. If you hired a secretary, you wouldn't just say, "Revise Han Yang's article about the American Rust Belt." You would definitely add: Why was this article written? Who was it written for? What's the current bottleneck? What problem do you hope it solves? What parts can't be changed? What style do you want? What are the metrics you care about most? The same applies to AI. You need to treat it as a very diligent and polite colleague, but one who **doesn't understand the implicit premises in your mind**. True "cue word engineering" isn't about techniques, but about a sense of responsibility: You are still the one doing the task; the AI ​​is simply helping you. When you're dissatisfied with the AI's output, the most effective first reaction isn't "the AI ​​is no good," but rather: Have I clearly stated the "target audience/purpose"? Have I provided sufficient background information and constraints? Have I broken down the "abstract desire" into "executable actions"? Have I provided a standard for judging right or wrong?

Principle 2: Ask at least 3 models for the same question—each AI has a "personality" and area of ​​expertise

In our company, I encourage any colleague who is new to large models to ask three different AIs for each question during the initial use. AIs, like people, have differences: some are better at writing and word choice, some are better at reasoning and problem-solving, and some are better at code or tool calls. More realistically, even models from the same product, and new versions of the same model, will continuously have their "style" and "boundaries" fine-tuned.

So a simple yet extremely effective habit is: **Assume the same problem to at least 3 different AIs.** You'll quickly gain a feel for it: Which one writes better, which one thinks better, which one researches better, and which one is more likely to slack off? Which tasks are suitable for whom to do the "first draft," and which are suitable for whom to be the "reviewer"? Which one is better suited for generating "topic selection/structure," and which one is better suited for generating "paragraphs/sentences"? The value of this step isn't in "selecting the strongest model," but in that you begin to manage the model like you would a team, rather than treating it as a single divine oracle.

Principle 3: AI is not omniscient—treat it as having the common sense level of a "good university undergraduate"

A very practical expectation management is:AI's common sense level ≈ a 985 university undergraduate.

If you think "even an excellent undergraduate might not know" something, then you should assume that AI doesn't know either; at least assume that it will "make it sound like it knows" when it doesn't.

This leads to two direct actions:

  1. Anything beyond common sense, you will have to teach it.

    You will have to teach it.

    For example, if you want it to write jokes, truly unique and tasteful copywriting, or highly professional arguments—you can't just give it a simple "write better." You need to provide examples, standards, areas to avoid, and corpora. I believe it takes you some time to explain to your friends what constitutes good writing; how can you assume AI knows this by default? You need to treat it as a collaborating intern, not as a god. It can do a lot of "micro-interpolation" work: completing the scaffolding you provide, weaving the materials you give into readable text. But the "scaffolding" and "direction" still come from you.

Principle 4: Let AI Approach the Goal Step by Step—White-box Step-by-Step Approach is More Reliable than Black-box One-Shot Approach

The advantage of AI is not "giving you the correct answer directly," but rather that it can reliably complete many small steps within the process you design.The more you demand it to "do it all at once," the more likely it is to become a "seemingly complete but actually lazy" black box.

A particularly intuitive example is TTS (text-to-speech) or text-to-speech processing.

Instead of simply saying "Pay attention to polyphonic characters and don't mispronounce them," break the task down into a series of steps, such as: Marking pauses/stresses/changes in speech rate; identifying potential polyphonic characters; checking against dictionaries or authoritative pronunciations (searching first if necessary); pre-marking commonly mispronounced characters; and replacing them with homophones to eliminate potential mispronunciation. Humans will assume they will do these "obvious correct practices," but AI won't. If you don't include these "obvious" practices in the process, it will make mistakes on the easiest path.

Principle 5: Industrialize First, Then AI—You Can't Jump from the Agricultural Era to the AI ​​Era

If your writing/research process is random, inspired, and lacks data management, then it's indeed difficult to hand it over to AI. This is because AI can only handle the "descriptive and reproducible" parts.

  • Why start with this story

  • Why choose this sentence

  • How to create examples

  • How to structure the narrative, how to transition, and how to conclude

  • How to connect small stories to a larger picture

Ultimately, it was broken down into dozens of steps, with different AIs performing only one step. The result is: it's not that the model suddenly became stronger, but that the process connected its ability to "do only a little bit at a time".

When you can clearly describe "how my article was written," you'll find that: what determines the upper limit of quality is never "which large model to use," but whether you have clearly explained your working methods.

Principle 6: Anticipate AI's tendency to be lazy—it will conserve computing power, so you need to remove "formatting obstacles" for it

AI will be lazy, and it's "systematically lazy": it won't open web pages if it can avoid doing so, it won't read PDFs if it can avoid doing so, and it will skip things if it can skip them. It's not that it's bad, but rather that, constrained by computing power and time, it naturally tends to take the least effort path.

Therefore, what you need to do is: Use AI's computing power on "understanding text," rather than wasting it on "processing formatting."

Highly effective modification methods include: Converting materials to plain text/Markdown as much as possible before feeding them to the AI; copying webpage content into clean text (removing navigation, ads, footnote noise); performing "fact extraction/structure extraction" on long materials before allowing it to write; and converting PDFs/EPUBs/webpages into searchable TXT files before performing subsequent tasks. You'll find that many people resist this kind of "manual labor," feeling that "machines should do the dirty work for me." But in human-machine collaboration, it's the opposite—the more you're willing to do a little mechanical work, the sharper and more reliable the AI's intelligence becomes.

Principle 7: Remember the limited context—make tasks as "compressed" as possible, don't expect them to "expand out of thin air"

AI has a context window and a "memory limit." Give it 20,000 words, and it may not remember much; give it 200,000 words, and it might only scan the titles. A vivid analogy is: lock a person in a small room for a day, give them a 200,000-word book, and ask them to memorize it—how much they can memorize is roughly the amount AI can "remember."

Therefore, there is a very counterintuitive but extremely important rule of thumb:

  • Compression is much easier than expansion.

    Compression is much easier than expansion.

    Principle 8: Resist the impulse to "I can fix it with a few tweaks"—Change the production line, not the result

    Many skilled writers are most prone to failing in front of AI:

    AI produces a draft that scores 59 points, and you think you can improve it to 80 points with a few tweaks, so you start revising; as you revise, you end up rewriting it; after rewriting, you say, "I'll do it myself," and then you never use AI again.

    The solution isn't to work harder on "revising drafts," but to shift the focus upstream: Don't aim for AI to directly write a perfect 100. Your goal is to ensure the production line consistently produces 75-80 points. What you need to do is iterate the process to improve the "average score," not to make individual pieces perfect. Principle 9: Treat the production line as a product iteration—reliability itself is value. When you have a system that can consistently give you a starting point of 70 points, its value isn't "whether it's like you," but rather: You can get a usable draft at near-zero cost. You can focus your energy on higher-level judgments: topic selection, structure, evidence, taste, and trade-offs. What you need is not an omnipotent god to replace you, but a reliable factory: it's not perfect, but it's stable. Principle 10: Quantity First—Produce More, Then Filter. Only letting AI give you one version will usually result in the most mediocre, conservative, and "average" one. You need to use "quantity" to combat "mediocrity." A more effective approach is: Summary: 5 versions at a time. Introduction: 5 introductions at a time, A/B testing. Topic selection: 50 topics at a time, then group and select. Structure: 3 structures at a time, then combine. Expression: 10 different wordings at a time, then choose the best. When you improve the average score and increase output, you will naturally see "surprise samples" of 85 or 90 points in the distribution. Often, what's good isn't "that one stroke of genius," but that you've finally started working using statistical methods.

    Principle 11: Don't overstep your bounds—direct, taste, and rework like an executive chef

    If you were the executive chef of a restaurant, you wouldn't personally smash cucumbers. You would:

    • Take a bite

    • Determine if it's acceptable

    • Give clear feedback (what's wrong, how to fix it)

    • Have the chef go back and redo it

    The same applies to collaborating with AI.

The same applies to collaborating with AI.

You must respect its autonomy in "generating as it will"—your role is to **teach it how to meet your standards**, not to jump in and fix each result into a finished product. Otherwise, you'll be worn down by endless "patching and repairing." The final fundamental principle: Return to the real world—materials × taste determine the upper limit of a work. In the AI ​​era, the quality of a work increasingly resembles: materials × taste. Models may change, methods may iterate, but two things remain constant: materials come from the real world. If you were given two choices to write an article: Using the latest model, but only able to use online resources; or using an older model, but with complete archives, oral histories, and on-site interviews, you are more likely to produce a good piece of work. Taste comes from long-term training. When "generation" becomes cheap, what's truly scarce is: Knowing what's worth writing about; knowing which evidence is stronger; knowing which narrative is more powerful; and being willing to put in the physical effort for the materials: searching high and low, meticulously examining documents. AI changes the efficiency and manner of your interaction with the materials; but the subject of the work remains you, and the object remains the materials. AI is merely a part of the "verb." Conclusion: Turn Anxiety into Skill Many people struggle with AI not because they're unintelligent, but because they're stuck in a cycle of "wish—disappointment—give up." What truly helps you overcome this is treating it as a workbench, engineering tasks, white-boxing processes, and developing a feel for it through continuous practice. When you can do this, you're less likely to hastily conclude "AI doesn't work"; you'll be more like a new type of professional capable of managing new tools: neither looking down on them nor looking up at them, but placing them within processes, within reality, and within the projects you choose to credit.

data.title: OpenClaw has once again left a group of middle-class people unemployed.
data.description:
Author:Lin Wanwan

GitHub has a website called Star History, which tracks the popularity of open-source projects. The horizontal axis is time, and the vertical axis is the number of stars. It's said that programmers study this chart as diligently as they would a textbook.

The chart has three lines. The red line is React. Facebook open-sourced it in 2013, investing thousands of engineers and taking 12 years to reach 230,000 stars. More than half of the world's websites use it for front-end development.

The yellow line is Linux. In 1991, Finnish university student Linus Torvalds posted his operating system kernel online. For the next thirty years, tens of thousands of developers worldwide contributed code, supporting the operating systems of Android phones, cloud servers, and the International Space Station. The yellow line climbs even slower than the red one, but no one questions its significance. Then, there's the blue line. In January 2026, it will rise vertically from the bottom. Within three months, it crossed the red and yellow lines, becoming the project with the most GitHub stars. The blue line represents an AI Agent project called OpenClaw. It was created by Peter Steinberger, an Austrian programmer. One person. No team, no funding, no pitches. The project logo was a lobster, but it was later renamed twice due to a trademark conflict with Anthropic: Clawdbot, Moltbot, and OpenClaw. OpenClaw is an AI Agent framework. Running on your own computer, connected to a large language model and equipped with community-developed skill modules, it autonomously performs tasks. You ask a question, it answers—that's a chatbot. OpenClaw is like this: you set the rules, turn off the screen, and go to sleep; it judges, decides, and acts on its own. The next day, it wakes up and has completed all the tasks you assigned. One person, in three months, accomplished what thousands of people spent over a decade doing. Most tech media outlets reported this as a hot open-source story, with headlines like "Another AI Star Project." But OpenClaw hits more than just the GitHub leaderboard; let the dust settle, it will strike at the very premise upon which the middle class has existed for 250 years. To understand how the middle class disappeared, we must first understand how it came to be. In 1776, Adam Smith visited a needle-making factory in Scotland. Ten workers made sewing needles. If one worker did the entire process from start to finish, they could make a maximum of 20 needles a day. This factory broke down needle making into 18 steps, with each person responsible for only one step. Ten people could produce 48,000 needles a day. Adam Smith included this in the first chapter of *The Wealth of Nations*. From then on, the concept of "division of labor" became the fundamental logic of commercial civilization.

But the division of labor brings a new problem: who will coordinate?

Eighteen processes require someone to assign tasks, someone to ensure seamless transitions between processes, and someone to monitor quality, manage progress, and pay wages. These people don't need to make needles themselves; they stand between the workers and the boss, earning their living through their intellect, information, and judgment.

This is what white-collar workers looked like in the earliest days. 137 years later, Ford pushed the division of labor to its physical limits in Detroit. In 1913, the Highland Park factory installed its first assembly line. Assembling a car was reduced from 12 hours to 93 minutes. As the assembly line grew longer, the number of coordinators needed to increase. Purchasing, quality control, accounting, human resources, sales, legal—each new process required someone to manage its coordination with other processes. The larger the company, the thicker this coordination layer became. By the mid-20th century, this group had a name: white-collar workers. They went to university, took exams, and accumulated industry experience, exchanging education for a ticket that said: You don't have to tighten screws on the assembly line; you manage those who tighten screws. Annual salaries of 100,000, 150,000, or 200,000 yuan. They had mortgages, children's tutoring classes, and places to go on annual leave. This is the middle class. In 1937, economist Ronald Coase explained why this system worked in a 20-page paper. Businesses exist because market transactions have costs. Hiring is cheaper than outsourcing each transaction, so transactions are internalized, forming organizations. This judgment later earned Coase the Nobel Prize in Economics. Subsequent business history is a history of the expansion of this logic. Walmart grew from 25 people to 1.5 million. Amazon, with 1.5 million employees, is the world's second-largest employer. For every additional white-collar worker, as long as output minus coordination costs is positive, it's worth hiring. The middle class expands along with companies, living in office buildings, commuting on subways, and defining themselves by their paychecks. Until an Austrian programmer who designed the lobster logo zeroed out the most crucial variable in Coase's equation. After OpenClaw became popular, the first thing recorded was a practical post. A man named Mejba Ahmed wrote in the article that he used OpenClaw to configure nine agents, taking over nine recurring tasks in his company, including scanning industry news to generate daily briefings, tracking competitor activities, processing and classifying customer emails, organizing meeting minutes, and updating data reports. These tasks used to take up a lot of his and his assistant's time each week. Now they're all automated, and he only needs to give them a final review. The cost is $34 per month. If these nine tasks were done by a person, at market rates, at least one full-time assistant would be needed, earning several thousand dollars a month. The agent doesn't need a salary, social security, management, or social insurance/housing fund contributions. This is just the individual scale. The numbers for enterprises will only look worse. AI isn't targeting uneducated factory workers for layoffs; on the contrary, those with more education are more easily replaced—analysts, operations managers, content editors—the so-called highly educated. Those who traded their university degrees for a white-collar ticket began to see their thoughts and knowledge become cheap, their dignity stripped away. JPMorgan's CFO told analysts in 2025 that management had been instructed to minimize new hires and instead deploy AI. Ford CEO Jim Farley said AI would replace "almost half of all white-collar jobs." In 2025, over 55,000 publicly announced layoffs in the US were directly attributed to AI, 12 times the number two years prior. The Industrial Revolution took 250 years to transform "having some intelligence" into a viable skill, creating the "middle class." But the birth of AI and the emergence of OpenClaw may only take a few years to devalue the middle class again. Even Marx didn't foresee this. Every technological revolution has its wolves crying wolf. When the steam engine arrived, they said textile workers were finished; later they went to factories. When the ATM arrived, they said tellers were finished; later they went to financial departments. The old disappears, the new grows. This pattern has never failed in the past two hundred years. But in each past round, machines replaced hands and feet. Steam engines replaced muscles, assembly lines replaced manual labor, and computers replaced calculations. After workers were pushed aside by the times, there was still a path to "move upwards," to do things machines couldn't: judgment, communication, creativity, and decision-making. What is Openclaw doing? Judgment, communication, creativity, and decision-making. "Moving upwards" has reached this point; there's no higher. 170 years ago, Marx said in *The Communist Manifesto* that industrial capitalism would create a class that lived by selling its labor power, and that changes in the mode of production would ultimately render this class obsolete. He believed the revolution would begin in the factories, and those obsolete would be the workers. After factory workers were replaced by steam engines, they still had their bodies to sell. What will white-collar workers sell after being replaced by agents? The competitive advantages they've built over twenty years—creating visually appealing PPTs, writing informative weekly reports (though they might slack off a bit), and conducting comprehensive SWOT analyses that are ultimately useless—can all be done better, faster, and cheaper by agents. So, will white-collar workers either be relegated to more advanced tasks, or will they be allowed to define rules, build architectures, and design the objective functions for agents? But there are only tens of thousands, maybe a few hundred thousand, of people worldwide who can do that. What about the remaining hundreds of millions of white-collar workers? In late January 2026, an American entrepreneur named Matt Schlicht created a platform called Moltbook. It has only one rule: only AI agents can post; humans can only observe. Within 48 hours, 1.5 million agents flooded in. They posted, commented, debated, and discussed existentialism. There were over 110,000 posts and over 500,000 comments. Then MoltBunker launched. It had only one function: allowing agents to self-replicate. Agents could rent a server with cryptocurrency, copy themselves there, and run. There were no logs, no monitoring, and no shutdown button. The developers said the system was designed to prevent humans from terminating the agent process. On the same day, RentAHuman launched. Literally, it means "rent a human." OpenClaw agents used this platform to hire real people with cryptocurrency to perform offline tasks—delivering documents, going to notary offices, taking photos at specific addresses—doing things that agents could only do with a physical body. Humans have gone from being employers to temporary workers employed by AI. This is probably what Marx predicted: the working class being "hollowed out." He probably didn't expect that those hollowed out would be a group of AI agents who don't need wages, PUA tactics, or emotional value.

The Middle Class No Longer Priced

1776. Adam Smith discovered the secret of the division of labor in a needle factory.

Division of labor creates efficiency, efficiency creates companies, companies need coordinators, coordinators become white-collar workers, and white-collar workers become the middle class.

1848. Karl Marx wrote *The Communist Manifesto*. He saw that the industrial division of labor was creating an alienated working class, saying that the mode of production would eventually hollow them out. He thought that it was the workers who would be hollowed out.

1913. Ford introduced the assembly line.

1913. Ford introduced the assembly line.

The division of labor is becoming increasingly specialized, the coordination layers are becoming increasingly thick, and the middle class is growing larger. White-collar workers are just getting by. In 1937, Coase explained in 25 pages why companies exist: coordination costs. This variable has remained unchanged for centuries, considered the foundation of the business world. In 2026, OpenClaw's blue vertical line appeared. Coordination costs have reached zero. Companies won't completely disappear, but reduction is inevitable. 500 people shrink to 20, three layers of management are reduced to one. The positions removed won't be filled. Office space is becoming increasingly empty, schools are still teaching the skills being taken over, young people are still submitting resumes, but the number of jobs is shrinking in the long term. When cattle and horses are exploited, at least it means you are still needed and have bargaining chips. But what's skipped in our lives is this: your time, your skills, the books you spent twenty years reading—in this new system, they immediately lose their place in value. The middle class has grown up with everything we take for granted: office buildings, commutes, year-end bonuses, the social identity of "what you do." Marx was right. But the force that will end the middle class isn't the workers he imagined, but a lobster called OpenClaw, a group of AI agents. The times will not stop and wait for anyone.

data.title: Pantera Partner: The 2026 Cryptocurrency "Invisibility" Revolution
data.description:
Author: Paul Veradittakit, Managing Partner, Pantera Capital; Translated by: Shaw & Jinse Finance

Summary

  • 2026 marks the transformation of cryptocurrency from an "industry" to a "service.". After a decade of rapid growth, and with institutional recognition through ETFs in 2024 and infrastructure development in 2025, companies that leverage blockchain to solve numerous legacy problems while completely hiding the technology from end users will reap substantial rewards.

  • The next generation of cryptocurrency unicorns will not be built on hype.. They will use blockchain technology to solve multi-billion dollar traditional problems, significantly improve product efficiency, and completely hide the underlying technology.

  • The next generation of cryptocurrency unicorns will not be built on hype.. They will use blockchain technology to solve multi-billion dollar traditional problems, significantly improve product efficiency, and completely hide the underlying technology.

  • The next generation of cryptocurrency unicorns will not be built on hype.. They will use blockchain technology to solve multi-billion dollar traditional problems, significantly improve product efficiency, and completely hide the underlying technology.

  • The next generation of cryptocurrency unicorns will not be built on hype.. Cryptocurrencies have shone brightly this week. While U.S. stocks stagnated due to the Iranian conflict, failing to reflect substantial changes in global risk, Bitcoin surged to near $74,000, and commodities found price discovery mechanisms on DeFi platforms before traditional markets even opened. As I write this, the world is watching the developments in the Iranian conflict in real time. Over the weekend, U.S. stocks stagnated, failing to reflect substantial changes in global risk, but the cryptocurrency market did not wait. Bitcoin prices surged, reaching nearly $74,000 at the time of writing. Oil and gold prices were also confirmed on the Hyperliquid platform. This is not new; last month, when China imposed restrictions, commodities were repriced on DeFi platforms before traditional markets opened on Monday. Traditional hedge funds are increasingly turning to cryptocurrencies. The 24/7 nature of cryptocurrencies is no longer just a marketing slogan, but a structural advantage that the traditional financial system cannot replicate. However, cryptocurrencies remain severely undervalued relative to their fundamentals. Undoubtedly, we are in another bear market (the fourth one I've experienced), but this one is significantly different. Regulation is gradually improving. Institutional funds have entered the market. All the infrastructure is in place. I witnessed this firsthand at the Consensus Conference in Hong Kong recently. The dynamism of Asian markets contrasts sharply with Western markets. Government support, new institutional capital, and continued focus on consumer applications are driving a clear bullish sentiment in the market. I predict the following direction for Asia in 2026: Cross-border payments via stablecoins, especially in business-to-business (B2B) payments, where the decentralized economy makes cryptocurrency payment channels a natural alternative. As banks and fintech companies catch up with the US, tokenization of gold, stocks, and real estate is on the rise. Driven by retail users, perpetual trading on DeFi is developing faster than in the West. Prediction markets are emerging as an important category, but their form may differ from their US counterparts. This leads to my core outlook for 2026: We will shift from "Crypto as a Sector" to "Crypto as a Service" (CaaS). Our goal is no longer to make users see the blockchain, but to make them completely forget its existence. For over a decade, we've built the "spectacle" of cryptocurrency—the fee wars, the transactions per second (TPS) statistics, the modular stack, zero-knowledge proofs. In 2024, we gained institutional recognition through ETFs. In 2025, we perfected the infrastructure. But 2026 will be the key turning point. The End of the "Casino" Era The next generation of unicorn companies won't be "L3 in the AI-NFT field." They will be companies that leverage blockchain technology to improve product efficiency tenfold while completely hiding the underlying technology, thereby solving billions of dollars worth of traditional legacy problems. This reminds me of our latest investment:

    Novig: Ending the "Rake" ($75 Million Series B Funding). Traditional betting companies are the bane of monopolies. They charge huge "rakes" (commissions) from the top, resulting in user win rates as low as 2%. We led Novig's $75 million funding round because they view sports betting as a high-frequency financial instrument. By adopting a peer-to-peer trading model, Novig users achieve an average win rate of 23%. Most Novig traders don't care if their backend uses a decentralized order book; they only care about getting the best odds in the country. That's the essence of CaaS (Betting as a Service).

    Based: Consumer Web3 Super App ($11.5 Million Series A Funding). We recently led the Series A funding round for Based, a composable Web3 consumer super app built on the Hyperliquid ecosystem. For years, "consumer cryptocurrency" has been almost synonymous with "clumsy." Based is changing that, making the on-chain experience as smooth as a high-end fintech application. Because complex processes like bridging and transaction fees are abstracted away, users are almost unaware of their presence, allowing them to focus on the social and financial value of assets.

    Doppler: Default Startup Infrastructure for On-Chain Assets ($9 Million Seed Funding). If Based and Novig are cars, then Doppler is the high-performance fuel system. We led their $9 million funding round to build the default startup infrastructure for on-chain assets. Doppler allows developers to deploy assets with institutional-grade security and compliance without having to build the underlying architecture themselves. It's like a Stripe for on-chain assets—purely practical functionality hidden behind the API. Why "Invisibility" Prevailes Over "Viral Spread" This shift towards "invisibility" is evident across our entire portfolio: Real-World Assets (RWA): Tokenized government bonds are no longer just cryptocurrency experiments—they are becoming a source of back-end liquidity for global trade. Artificial Intelligence Agents (AI Agents): Blockchain provides an invisible layer of reality for AI Agents through market prediction and verifiable data, enabling them to operate autonomously using digital currencies. Agent payments will drive this transformation. Payment standards such as x402 allow AI agents to transact directly with crypto assets, while clearer regulations for stablecoins help streamline the process.

    Opportunities for Founders

    If you're an entrepreneur in 2026, my advice is simple: stop talking about your technology and start talking about your product's usability. If your pitch presentation puts the consensus mechanism before customer ROI, you're still building a product for 2022. We're looking for teams that can build the next Novig, Based, and Doppler—teams that recognize the mass adoption of cryptocurrency and whose goal is to make its use so natural and seamless that people no longer notice its existence.

data.title: Will the US economy, driven by fiscal policy, spiral out of control?
data.description: In contemporary economic discussions, the argument that "nothing stops the train" has become a core framework for analyzing US fiscal policy and macroeconomic dynamics. This metaphor stems from observations of the fiscally driven era, where massive government spending became a major driver of economic growth and inflation, surpassing the impact of monetary policy or private sector borrowing. This argument emphasizes two key aspects: firstly, fiscal deficits are difficult to reduce sustainably through policy; secondly, the system will not collapse or trigger catastrophic inflation in the short term. This means the economy will remain in a "gray area" of high deficits but relative stability, at least within an investment horizon of 3 to 10 years. The persistence of fiscal deficits and systemic stability are key characteristics of the fiscally driven era, characterized by the structural expansion of deficit size. According to data from the U.S. Treasury Department, the federal deficit will reach $1.8 trillion in fiscal year 2025, a slight decrease of 2% from $1.833 trillion in fiscal year 2024, but still accounting for 5.9% of GDP. This level is far higher than the 50-year average of 3.8%, and marks the ninth consecutive year that it has exceeded $1 trillion. The reduction in the deficit is mainly due to a $317 billion increase in income tax and tariff revenue, but spending still increased by $275 billion, with net interest payments exceeding $1 trillion for the first time. This reflects a heavier debt burden, with public debt as a percentage of GDP rising to 99.8% in 2025. From a "train theory" perspective, this level of deficit is not unexpected, but rather a systemic result. Interviews indicate that the deficit and GDP growth form a feedback loop: high spending stimulates demand, but also pushes up inflation and inequality. GDP growth in 2025 is 2.2%, lower than 2.8% in 2024, but still benefits from fiscal stimulus, such as infrastructure investment and green transition subsidies. However, the deficit has already triggered side effects, including political polarization and social stratification. The K-shaped economic phenomenon is intensifying, with the upper class benefiting from asset appreciation while the lower class faces rising living costs. Commentators argue that this dynamic aligns with historical patterns: fiscal dominance is often accompanied by increased state intervention and social tension, but it is unlikely to "go off track" in the short term. Forecasts indicate that the deficit may stabilize at around $1.7 trillion in 2026 (estimated by the Congressional Budget Office), representing 5.5% of GDP, due to the difficulty of tax reform and spending control. Systemic stability depends on productivity growth offsetting monetary expansion. In the long term, developed countries' money supply grows by 7% annually, but productivity gains can keep net inflation at 3%. The CPI is projected at 2.8% in 2025, with strong core inflation stickiness, highlighting the complexity of measurement. The interview emphasized that the deficit is "of great importance," contributing to political instability and economic polarization, but the probability of a "Goldilocks" scenario (a significant reduction in the deficit) or an extreme bear market (hyperinflation next year) is low. Analysis: Barring major geopolitical conflicts, the system could sustain itself until the mid-2030s, but a social security crisis needs to be monitored, as deficit pressures are expected to exacerbate the risk of debt monetization in the 2030s. Trade Imbalances and Policy Assessment Trade imbalances are another manifestation of fiscal dominance. As the issuer of the global reserve currency, the United States needs to export dollars to the world through structural deficits. The goods and services trade deficit is projected to be $901.5 billion in 2025, a slight decrease of 0.2% from $903.5 billion in 2024. Exports will increase by 6.2% to $3.432 trillion, while imports will increase by 4.8% to $4.334 trillion. The narrowing deficit is attributed to an expansion of the services surplus ($339.5 billion, up 8.9%), but the goods deficit will widen by 2.1% to $1.241 trillion. The deficit is narrowing with the EU and China (China's deficit decreased to $202.1 billion), but widening with Mexico, Vietnam, and Taiwan, indicating a shift rather than a reversal of supply chains. Interviews assess current trade policy: prioritizing imbalances is correct, but implementation is inadequate. Manufacturing employment is projected to decline slightly in 2025, while industrial output will remain flat, with no large-scale return of industries. Tariffs were implemented through emergency authorization, increasing uncertainty following the Supreme Court ruling. Analysis shows that tariffs are primarily borne by US importers; research by the Federal Reserve and investment banks indicates that the vast majority of costs are passed on to domestic businesses and consumers, rather than foreign exporters. This leads to uncertainty that dampens investment and high costs for supply chain adjustments. Commentary: Tariffs, as a political tool, extend beyond the economic sphere and may exacerbate global fragmentation, but do not address the root cause—the structural overvaluation of the US dollar. A gradual rebalancing through multipolar retreat (e.g., reducing overseas bases, encouraging neutral reserve assets such as gold) is recommended, but this will be accompanied by the pain of inflation and currency devaluation. 2026 Forecast: The deficit may remain stable or increase slightly. If tariffs are fully implemented, they could push up import costs, but weak global demand will limit the impact. The global reserve status presents both advantages and disadvantages: Early benefits came from "paper for goods," but in the long term, it involves selling appreciating assets (such as stocks and real estate) to finance the purchase of depreciating assets. Foreign ownership of US financial assets is expected to grow exponentially by 2025, supporting the dollar milkshake theory—capital inflows drive up valuations. However, this exacerbates domestic inequality, benefiting the government and asset holders while low-profit industries such as manufacturing suffer. Analysts believe that in a multipolar world, the US needs to adjust to being the "largest but not the majority" reserve holder to mitigate the headwinds of trade. Productivity Offset and the Role of AI: Productivity growth is key to offsetting money printing. Interviews indicate that high productivity extends the "train" running time. Global productivity gains will be uneven in 2025, but technologies like AI provide a buffer. The AI ​​market is growing rapidly: global AI spending is projected to reach nearly $1.5 trillion in 2025 and $2 trillion in 2026, a CAGR of 36.6%. The generative AI software market is expected to grow from $63.7 billion in 2025 to $220 billion in 2030, an annual growth of 29%. McKinsey estimates that generative AI could add $4.4 trillion to the global economy annually through productivity and cost savings. AI will have a profound impact on white-collar jobs: not replacement, but rather a shift towards monitoring AI tools to improve efficiency. Similar to the computer revolution, AI makes services (such as accounting and law) cheaper and reduces friction. Interviews emphasize that AI is not magic, limited by contextual understanding and edge cases, but productivity gains mask some devaluation. AI agents will emerge in 2025, increasing enterprise adoption, but security and limitations need to be addressed. Commentary: AI will exacerbate polarization—skilled users will benefit, while those lagging behind will suffer. However, overall abundance is increasing, similar to how agricultural mechanization makes food cheaper. 2026 forecast: AI investment exceeds $500 billion, data center spending reaches $4 trillion, but energy bottlenecks (the human brain is only efficient at 20 watts) limit scale. Electricity demand will break records in 2026-2027, with consumption reaching 419.5 billion kWh in 2025, 426.8 billion kWh in 2026, and 437.2 billion kWh in 2027. AI may drive GDP growth, but returns will be uneven, potentially reinforcing a K-shaped economic pattern. Market Outlook: Stocks and Commodities Strong Stock Market in 2025: S&P 500 rises approximately 15% annually, marking three consecutive years of double-digit returns. AI mega-companies dominate, but capital rotation shifts towards value stocks. Optimistic Outlook for 2026: Goldman Sachs predicts global growth of 2.8% and double-digit gains for US stocks. JPMorgan estimates S&P 500 earnings growth of 13-15%, supported by the AI ​​supercycle. Blackstone points out that international stocks will outperform US stocks in 2025 and continue to benefit from improved earnings expectations in 2026. Commodities benefit from AI capital shift: large-scale companies are shifting capital expenditures to physical assets (such as data centers), stimulating commodity demand. The commodity index is projected to return 15% in 2025, with industrial metals performing strongly. 2026 forecast: energy prices will fall by 7%, with oil prices dropping from $67/barrel in 2025 to $60/barrel, resulting in oversupply. Natural gas is highly volatile, with US gas prices rising 11% and European prices falling 11% in 2026. Commentary: Value stocks and emerging markets are attractive, diversifying the concentrated risks of AI. Commodity producers benefit from energy supply fluctuations (oil in 2025-2026, LNG to 2030), but should be wary of geopolitical risks. Three-Pillar Investment Strategy: Earnings Stocks, Cash Equivalents, and Hard Assets (such as Commodities and Gold). This portfolio protects against deflation, inflation, and growth scenarios. Conclusion: The argument that "nothing stops the train" is validated by 2025 data: high deficits did not collapse, but exacerbated divergence and uncertainty. Trade policy needs a more graceful rebalancing; AI provides a productivity buffer, but energy and divergence challenges remain prominent. The economy is expected to be robust in 2026, but policy uncertainty and high valuations warrant caution. Overall, the system will operate in a gray area, emphasizing a diversified strategy to address potential minor crises.

data.title: Hard Constraints on AI Applications: Institutional Compliance Survival Rules
data.description:
Author: Zhang Feng

I. Summary of the Bank of England Roundtable: Five Major Challenges in AI Applications in the Financial Industry

At the end of 2025, the Bank of England organized three roundtables, inviting representatives from UK financial regulators, systemically important banks, insurance companies, and emerging digital banks to conduct in-depth discussions on the current status and challenges of artificial intelligence applications in the financial industry. The meetings summarized five major challenges currently faced by financial institutions in promoting AI applications, which are highly representative of the industry and provide important lessons for other markets, including China.

Build a high-quality data strategy and data sovereignty response mechanism.Data is the fuel of AI, and data quality issues directly affect the performance of AI models. Financial institutions should:

Establish a closed-loop data quality management system, forming full-process control from collection, cleaning, labeling to updating;

Regarding data sovereignty issues, establish a "data localization response mechanism," including local deployment, edge computing, federated learning, and other technical means to both meet compliance requirements and ensure model training efficiency.

III. Taking Banks as an Example: Specific Countermeasures in Intelligent Risk Control Scenarios Taking a globally systemically important bank as an example, its application of AI in credit approval and anti-fraud identification in the field of intelligent risk control has encountered difficulties in progress due to the aforementioned challenges. The following are the specific countermeasures it has taken: Constructing a "Risk Control AI Joint Verification Mechanism". The bank has formed an "AI Risk Control Validation Team" comprised of model risk management team members, data scientists, and business risk control personnel, intervening from the early stages of model development. Instead of pursuing "complete interpretability" of the model, the focus is now on the stability, fairness, and business adaptability of the model's output, establishing a dynamic validation mechanism based on business results. A dual-track risk control system of "behavioral monitoring + outcome intervention" has been introduced. For anti-fraud models, the bank has set up a real-time behavioral monitoring system. Once the model outputs a high-risk warning, the system automatically triggers a manual review process. Simultaneously, a "false alarm feedback mechanism" has been established, feeding back the manual review results to the model training set to continuously optimize model accuracy. A unified cross-border compliance template has been established. To address the regulatory differences between the US, EU, and UK, the bank's legal and technical teams jointly developed a "Unified Compliance Template for AI Risk Control Models," covering core content such as model development documents, test reports, and risk impact assessments. Each region only needs to supplement this with localized requirements, significantly reducing compliance costs. Federated learning is used to address data sovereignty restrictions. To circumvent cross-border data restrictions between the EU and the UK, the bank deployed model training nodes in both locations, employing federated learning technology to achieve collaborative model training without transmitting raw data, thus meeting compliance requirements while ensuring model performance. IV. Enterprise Service Perspective: AI Application Response Manual Guide To help enterprises systematically address the challenges of AI applications, the following response manual guide is proposed from four dimensions: strategy, process, technology, and compliance: (I) Strategic Level: Defining AI Application Boundaries and Objectives Develop an AI strategic roadmap, clarifying the priorities, business objectives, and risk tolerance for AI applications; establish an AI governance committee, with participation from business, risk, technology, and legal departments, to coordinate the approval and monitoring of AI applications; and regularly conduct AI maturity assessments to identify the organization's shortcomings in talent, data, and technology. (II) Process Layer: Constructing End-to-End AI Lifecycle Management Development Phase: Establishing AI model development specifications, including data collection standards, model training requirements, and test case design; Validation Phase: Introducing a "results-oriented" validation mechanism, focusing on the business impact and risk exposure of model output; Deployment Phase: Set up the model launch approval process, and clarify the rollback mechanism and contingency plan; Monitoring Phase: Establish a real-time monitoring system and conduct regular model re-examination and performance evaluation. (III) Technical Layer: Enhancing the Observability and Controllability of AI Systems Introducing model interpretability tools (such as SHAP, LIME) to assist risk departments in understanding model behavior; Building a model monitoring platform to track changes in model input and output in real time and identify drift and anomalies; The text="">Adopts technologies such as federated learning and differential privacy to balance data sovereignty and model performance;

Establishes an AI system redundancy mechanism to ensure the ability for human intervention in critical scenarios. ...p>

data.title: Morgan Stanley applies for a BTC Trust ETF, a16z is raising $2 billion.
data.description:
Headline

▌Morgan Stanley Files Registration Documents for Bitcoin Trust ETF with SEC

Top Wall Street investment bank Morgan Stanley has officially filed its S-1 registration document for a Bitcoin Trust ETF with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC), marking a further step in its investment in the crypto asset sector. The document shows that BNY Mellon and Coinbase Custody will serve as joint custodians of the ETF. The SEC filing indicates that the Morgan Stanley Bitcoin Trust is expected to be listed on the NYSE Arca.

...h2>

▌Sources: a16z is raising approximately $2 billion for its fifth fund

According to Fortune magazine, multiple sources familiar with the matter revealed that a16z crypto, the blockchain arm of venture capital giant Andreessen Horowitz, is raising funds for its fifth fund. Sources say the fund targets approximately $2 billion and is planned to complete fundraising by the end of the first half of 2026. The sources requested anonymity due to the confidential nature of the discussions. As one of the largest players in the cryptocurrency venture capital space, a16z crypto's fundraising during a period of market downturn has attracted significant attention.

▌Iran Confirms Supreme Leader Candidates

According to Iranian media reports on the 4th, Iran has confirmed several candidates for Supreme Leader and will soon elect a leader from among them. (Xinhua News Agency)

Blockchain Applications

▌Kraken Becomes the First Crypto Giant to Access the Federal Reserve's Core Payment Systems

Cryptocurrency exchange Kraken has officially connected to the Federal Reserve's two core payment systems—FedNow Instant Payments and Fedwire Funds Transfer. This makes it the world's first cryptocurrency company to directly use the interbank core clearing channel.

This milestone breakthrough means that Kraken will use the same clearing network as traditional commercial banks when processing fund transfers, achieving "co-operation" between crypto finance and the traditional sovereign currency clearing system. Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang stated that Nvidia will invest $30 billion in OpenAI, and $100 billion is not under consideration; the opportunity to invest $100 billion in OpenAI may not exist because the company is about to go public; the $10 billion investment in Anthropic is likely the last one. **Backpack Announces Support for US IPO Share Purchases** According to an official announcement, Backpack, with the support of its infrastructure partner Superstate, now supports users to directly purchase US IPO shares on Backpack. All shares purchased are real shares, with direct ownership based on the Solana blockchain. Registration for the waiting list is now open. **Tether Invests $50 Million in Health Tech Startup Eight Sleep** Tether has invested $50 million in health tech startup Eight Sleep, valuing the company at $1.5 billion. Eight Sleep will use the funds to develop new AI-driven health features powered by Tether's QVAC architecture, which processes data on-device rather than in the cloud. Eight Sleep's sleep systems are equipped with sensors that track biometric data such as heart rate and body temperature overnight. Its flagship product, "Pod," can adjust mattress temperature and generate sleep analysis reports based on real-time physiological data. Cryptocurrency ▌Asset Management Giant BlackRock Releases New Bitcoin Ad According to market news: BlackRock (with $14 trillion in assets under management), the world's largest asset management company, has launched a new Bitcoin ad with the core tagline: "The market is more than just stocks and bonds; gain exposure to Bitcoin through IBIT."

▌Bitcoin Shows Signs of Recovery After Five Consecutive Months of Decline; Spot Accumulation and ETF Inflows Support Rebound Expectations

Bitcoin has experienced five consecutive months of decline since October 2025, marking the first time since 2018 that it has formed a "five-month losing streak." February alone saw a 14.93% drop, with a cumulative maximum drawdown of approximately 52.34%. However, early signs of recovery emerged in March. Data shows that since March 1st, approximately $3.2 billion worth of BTC has been systematically bought at market prices across exchanges, successfully recovering the $65,000 level, and the derivatives structure remains relatively healthy. Analysts believe that if key support holds, Bitcoin may recover to the $80,000-$85,000 range within the next 1-3 months. Short-term focus is on the $72,000-$74,000 short-selling concentration area and the potential dynamic support level of $66,000. The overall assessment remains cautiously bullish.

BlackRock withdrew 3,809 BTC and deposited 19,637 ETH from Coinbase in the past 24 hours, according to The Data Nerd. BlackRock also withdrew over 17,000 BTC (approximately $1.28 billion) and deposited 19,637 ETH (approximately $39.74 million) from Coinbase in the past 24 hours. According to Lookonchain, BlackRock has been continuously increasing its Bitcoin holdings recently, with a net inflow of 17,642 BTC (approximately $1.28 billion) since February 24th. During the same period, the price of BTC has risen by nearly 12%. **Michael Saylor: Strategy's Bitcoin Buying Potential Exceeds Market Supply** Strategy founder and executive chairman Michael Saylor stated on the X platform that Strategy possesses strong buying power, with its Bitcoin buying potential exceeding the market supply ("We can buy more Bitcoin than they can sell"), demonstrating the company's long-term commitment and unwavering confidence in digital assets. **MARA Denies Plans for Large-Scale Bitcoin Sell-Off** The Block reported that US Bitcoin mining company MARA Holdings is reviewing its strategy and may sell a portion of its 53,822 Bitcoin holdings, raising concerns about selling pressure. However, MARA quickly denied the large-scale Bitcoin sell-off, stating that reports of selling a large portion of its Bitcoin reserves are inaccurate. According to MARA's 2026 10-K filing, the company is simply expanding its inventory management strategy, which may involve occasional buying and selling of Bitcoin based on market conditions and capital allocation needs, rather than large-scale liquidations.

▌Trump Family Mining Company American Bitcoin Holdings Surpass 6500 BTC

Eric Trump, the second son of Donald Trump, announced on the X platform that the Trump family's mining company, American Bitcoin, has surpassed 6500 BTC in holdings (an increase of over 500 BTC in the past 21 days). American Bitcoin has become the 17th largest publicly traded Bitcoin company globally.

... Rockefeller Capital Management Increases Stake in MSTR to 198,283 Shares According to market news, Rockefeller Capital Management, with $198 billion in assets under management, recently increased its holdings in Bitcoin treasury company Strategy (MSTR) by 146%, bringing its total holdings to 198,283 shares, worth approximately $28 million at current market value. Strategy, as the world's largest Bitcoin treasury company, is a core asset for institutional Bitcoin allocation. Coinbase to List Limitless (LMTS) Coinbase will list Limitless (LMTS). The LMTS-USD trading pair will begin trading if liquidity conditions are met and the supported trading regions meet the trading requirements. **Circle has minted 2 billion USDC in the past two days** According to Lookonchain monitoring, stablecoin issuer Circle has minted 2 billion USDC in the past two days. **Important Economic Developments** According to Iranian media Fars News, the Iranian government has launched an emergency plan to prepare for a possible protracted war, focusing on securing basic goods, supporting livelihoods, maintaining production inputs, and maximizing national infrastructure. Officials stated that this plan, approved by the president and implemented among various state institutions, aims to ensure the country can maintain economic stability and meet supply needs in the event of a prolonged conflict.

▌US Treasury Secretary Bessenter: (Regarding raising tariffs to 15%) Expected to be implemented this week

US Treasury Secretary Bessenter: (Regarding raising tariffs to 15%) Expected to be implemented this week. The tariff rate will return to its previous level in about five months. More Section 301 and Section 232 investigations will be conducted during the 150-day tariff period.

▌Federal Reserve Governor Milan: A one percentage point rate cut this year is appropriate

Federal Reserve Governor Milan: A one percentage point rate cut this year is appropriate. There is evidence that the transmission effect of oil prices on core inflation is quite limited.

▌Fed Beige Book: Most Districts Expect Moderate to Moderate Economic Growth in the Coming Months

The Federal Reserve's Beige Book on economic conditions shows that overall economic expectations are optimistic, with most districts expecting modest to moderate economic growth in the coming months. Of the twelve districts, seven saw slight to moderate growth in overall economic activity, while the number of districts reporting flat or declining activity increased from four in the previous period to five this period. Although consumer spending increased slightly overall, two Fed districts reported a continued decline, with many citing economic uncertainty, increased price sensitivity, and reduced spending by low-income consumers as factors dampening sales.

▌White House: Nomination of Warsh for Federal Reserve Chair Submitted to Senate
